disease.name: Acute Limb Ischemia
disease.note: Sudden reduction in limb perfusion threatening tissue viability — a true vascular emergency. The 6 Ps: Pain, Pallor, Pulselessness, Paresthesia, Paralysis, Poikilothermia. In Kenya, causes include atrial fibrillation (embolic), peripheral arterial disease (thrombotic), and iatrogenic from arterial catheterization. Time-to-treatment is critical: muscle necrosis begins at 4-6 hours, nerve damage at 6-8 hours. Rutherford classification guides urgency. Fasciotomy after revascularization prevents compartment syndrome.
presentingComplaints: Leg has turned cold and pale
Cannot move the foot or toes
No pulse felt in the foot
Leg suddenly became very painful and useless

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Rutherford IIb-III (motor/sensory loss) -> SEVERE — emergency surgery; Rutherford I (viable) -> NON-SEVERE — urgent but can wait briefly

history_questions.question: When exactly did the leg pain start?
Did the pain come on suddenly or gradually?
Is the leg cold compared to the other side?
Can you feel me touching your foot and toes?
Can you move your foot and toes up and down?
Do you have any sensation in the foot at all?
Do you have atrial fibrillation or irregular heartbeat?
Have you had a heart attack or heart valve problem before?
Do you have known peripheral artery disease (PAD)?
Do you have diabetes or high blood pressure?
Do you smoke tobacco or chew miraa/khat?
Have you had any catheterization or angiogram recently?
Have you had any injury to the leg recently?
Have you ever had a DVT or blood clot?
Do you have any history of aortic aneurysm?
Are you on any blood thinners (warfarin, aspirin, clopidogrel)?
Have you noticed the urine being dark (Coca-Cola colored)?
Is the pain getting worse over time?
Have you had similar episodes before that resolved?
Do you have pain in the other leg when walking (claudication)?
Have you ever had a stroke or TIA (transient ischemic attack)?
Do you have any known clotting disorder (thrombophilia)?
Have you had any blood transfusions recently?
Do you take any herbal medications or traditional remedies?
Have you had any recent infections with high fever?
What is your work — do you sit or stand for long hours?
Have you recently had a long bus or matatu journey (>4 hours)?
Did you have leg swelling before the pain started?
Was your leg a normal color before this episode?
Do you have disease in your arm or hand arteries too?
Have you had any previous leg artery surgery?
Do you have any connective tissue disease (Marfan, Ehlers-Danlos)?
Do you have Buerger's disease or have you been told your blood vessels are inflamed?
Have you had COVID-19 recently — within the past 3 months?
Do you have any liver disease or cirrhosis?
Have you lost weight unintentionally in the past few months?
Do you have family history of blood clots, aneurysm, or sudden death?
How many pregnancies and deliveries have you had?
Do you use any recreational drugs (cocaine, amphetamines, marijuana)?
Has the leg changed color from pale to blue to black over time?
Is the pain constant or does it come and go in waves?
Do you have any pain in the other leg when resting in bed?
Have you had any imaging of your leg arteries before (ultrasound, CT, angiogram)?
Are you still able to walk and do your normal daily activities?

examination_findings.finding: Absent pulse distal to occlusion level
Cold limb with definite line of temperature change
Pallor or mottled skin appearance
Sensory loss — decreased or absent light touch and pinprick
Motor weakness or complete paralysis
Muscle tenderness on palpation (compartment syndrome)
Absent Doppler arterial signal
Venous Doppler signal may still be audible (differentiates from DVT)
Tense, swollen, shiny leg (compartment syndrome)
Pain on passive stretch of toes (compartment syndrome sign)
Atrial fibrillation on pulse exam
Femoral pulse may be absent if aortoiliac occlusion
Foot in fixed plantar flexion (late irreversible sign)
Foul smell or blistering (late gangrene)
Hypotension or tachycardia (if reperfusion syndrome developing)
Femoral pulse palpable? Check bilaterally — absent suggests aortoiliac occlusion
Popliteal pulse assessment — critical for surgical planning and level of occlusion
Dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulse grading (0=absent, 1=diminished, 2=normal, 3=bounding)
Dermatomal sensory mapping of the entire foot and lower leg
Muscle power grading (0-5) for ankle plantarflexion, dorsiflexion, toe movements
Capillary refill time — prolonged >3 seconds suggests severe ischemia
Venous filling time — delayed >20 seconds suggests significant arterial occlusion
Skin breakdown, blistering, or early gangrene at toes or pressure points
Calf muscle tenderness or firmness (assess for chronic compartment syndrome)
Contralateral popliteal aneurysm on palpation (embolic source from popliteal aneurysm)
Cardiovascular exam: JVP, heart sounds, murmurs — identify valvular source of embolism
Abdominal exam: palpate for aortic aneurysm as a proximal embolic source
Check the skin for signs of cholesterol emboli (livedo reticularis, blue toe)
Measure calf and thigh circumference bilaterally — asymmetry indicates acute swelling

bedside_scores.name: Rutherford Classification for Acute Limb Ischemia

components.name: Category I (Viable): no sensory loss, no motor loss, audible arterial Doppler
Category IIa (Marginally Threatened): sensory loss (toes), no motor loss, inaudible arterial Doppler
Category IIb (Immediately Threatened): sensory loss + motor loss (mild), inaudible arterial Doppler
Category III (Irreversible): profound sensory loss + paralysis, inaudible arterial and venous Doppler

bedside_scores.interpretation: Rutherford I: urgent revascularization within 24h; IIa: within 6-12h; IIb: emergency within 1-2h; III: primary amputation — no revascularization (reperfusion would cause fatal systemic effects)

riskFactors.note: Tobacco and khat use increase thrombotic risk

subtypes.name: Viable Limb (Rutherford I)
subtypes.management: Urgent anticoagulation (IV Heparin bolus 5,000 IU then 1,000-1,500 IU/h). Urgent CTA or arterial Duplex. Embolectomy (Fogarty catheter) for embolic occlusion. Catheter-directed thrombolysis for thrombotic occlusion (Alteplase 0.5-1mg/h for 12-24h). Treat underlying cause (AF: anticoagulate, cardioversion).

subtypes.name: Threatened Limb (Rutherford IIa/IIb)
subtypes.management: Emergency revascularization within 6h. IV Heparin. Embolectomy (Fogarty) for emboli. Surgical bypass if thrombotic with PAD. Catheter-directed thrombolysis if IIa and short occlusion. Fasciotomy (4-compartment) after reperfusion. Reperfusion syndrome management: IV fluids, NaHCO3, mannitol, monitor K+ and CK.

subtypes.name: Irreversible Limb (Rutherford III)
subtypes.management: Primary amputation. Do not attempt revascularization — reperfusion of necrotic tissue releases myoglobin, K+, and acid into systemic circulation causing cardiac arrest and renal failure. Urgent AK amputation level determined by clinical viability. Stump closure or guillotine amputation if infected.

bedside.name: Handheld Doppler assessment (arterial + venous signals)
bedside.indication: First-line: identify level of occlusion, assess severity

bedside.name: Ankle-Brachial Index (ABI)
bedside.indication: If obtainable — ABI <0.4 suggests severe ischemia

bedside.name: ECG
bedside.indication: Identify AF, recent MI as embolic source

bedside.name: Compartment pressure measurement
bedside.indication: If compartment syndrome suspected — pressure >30 mmHg = fasciotomy

laboratory.name: Creatinine, CK, Myoglobin
laboratory.indication: Rhabdomyolysis assessment — CK >5000 with renal risk

laboratory.name: Lactate
laboratory.indication: Tissue ischemia marker — >2 suggests significant ischemia

laboratory.name: Urea, Creatinine, eGFR
laboratory.indication: Renal function — contrast risk, rhabdomyolysis monitoring

imaging.name: Arterial Duplex Ultrasound
imaging.indication: Fast, bedside — locate occlusion, assess flow

imaging.name: CT Angiography (Lower Limb Runoff)
imaging.indication: Definitive imaging — shows occlusion level, collateral flow, run-off vessels

imaging.name: Digital Subtraction Angiography (DSA)
imaging.indication: Gold standard — can be combined with endovascular intervention (thrombolysis, angioplasty)

imaging.name: Echocardiogram (Transthoracic + Transesophageal)
imaging.indication: Source of embolism — cardiac thrombus, valvular vegetation, myxoma

management.protocolReference: ESVS Guidelines for Acute Limb Ischemia (2024)

resuscitation: Assess ABC — ALI can be due to aortic dissection, MI, or PE
IV Heparin bolus 5,000 IU (70 IU/kg) then infusion 1,000-1,500 IU/h — prevents thrombus propagation
IV fluids (Normal Saline or Ringer's Lactate) to maintain hydration
Urinary catheter — monitor output, target >0.5 mL/kg/h
analgesia: IV Morphine 2.5-5 mg titrated to pain

surgical: Fogarty catheter embolectomy: for embolic occlusion (AF, valvular) — local or GA, incision over common femoral artery, passage of Fogarty balloon catheter proximally and distally
Surgical bypass (femoro-popliteal, femoro-distal): for thrombotic occlusion on PAD
Fasciotomy: 4-compartment fasciotomy of leg (anterior, lateral, superficial posterior, deep posterior) after revascularization
endovascular: Catheter-directed thrombolysis (CDT): Alteplase 0.5-1mg/h for 12-24h via multi-sidehole catheter embedded in thrombus
Pharmacomechanical thrombectomy (PMT): AngioJet, Rotarex for rapid thrombus removal
Angioplasty + stenting: for underlying stenosis after thrombus removal

immediate: ICU/HDU admission — monitor for reperfusion syndrome
Compartment syndrome monitoring: hourly neurovascular checks
IV fluids aggressively (to maintain urine output >1 mL/kg/h)
NaHCO3 8.4% 50-100 mL IV if acidotic or to alkalinize urine
Mannitol 20% 100-200 mL IV if high CK or compartment concern
Monitor K+ hourly for first 6 hours — treat hyperkalemia aggressively
Check CK, lactate, K+, creatinine at 4h, 8h, 12h, 24h
day1_3: Continue IV heparin or transition to DOAC/Warfarin if embolic source
Monitor for compartment syndrome: if tense, swollen, or pain on stretch → re-measure compartment pressure → fasciotomy if >30 mmHg
Wound care (fasciotomy sites) — keep open, cover with moist saline gauze, delayed primary closure or skin graft at 5-7 days
DVT prophylaxis (if not already on heparin)
day3_7: Fasciotomy wound management: reassess for delayed closure
Plan definitive surgery if needed (bypass, aneurysm repair)
Anticoagulation plan for discharge: DOAC for AF, Warfarin for mechanical valve

pathways.criteria: no sensory/motor loss, audible arterial Doppler, capillary return present
pathways.treatment: IV Heparin. Urgent CTA. Embolectomy or thrombolysis (catheter-directed). Treat underlying cause (AF, PAD). Fasciotomy only if high risk.

pathways.criteria: sensory loss ± mild motor loss, no arterial Doppler signal, duration <6h
pathways.treatment: Emergency revascularization within 1-6h. IV Heparin. Embolectomy/bypass. 4-compartment fasciotomy after reperfusion. ICU for reperfusion syndrome management.

pathways.criteria: profound motor/sensory loss, rigor, fixed joints, absent venous and arterial Doppler, gangrene
pathways.treatment: Primary amputation. Do NOT revascularize. Guillotine amputation if infected, definitive closure later. Reperfusion would cause fatal systemic complications.

operative_templates.indication: Acute embolic occlusion of femoral/popliteal arteries with viable or threatened limb (Rutherford I-IIb)
operative_templates.approach: Transverse arteriotomy at common femoral artery bifurcation
operative_templates.position: Supine with leg slightly abducted and externally rotated
operative_templates.incision: Vertical groin incision over femoral pulse (or where pulse should be) — from inguinal ligament distally 6-8cm
steps: Groin incision through skin and subcutaneous tissue
Identify common femoral artery and its bifurcation into profunda femoris and superficial femoral artery
Control vessel proximally and distally with vessel loops
IV Heparin 5,000 IU given
Make transverse arteriotomy on anterior wall of common femoral artery
Pass Fogarty catheter (4F for distal, 5-6F for proximal) proximally (towards iliac/aorta)
Inflate balloon with 0.5-1.0 mL saline and withdraw gently — extract thrombus
Repeat until good proximal flow achieved (pulsatile bleeding)
Pass Fogarty catheter distally into SFA and extract distal thrombus
Repeat until good back-bleeding achieved
Heparinized saline flush into distal vessel
Close arteriotomy with 5-0 or 6-0 Prolene interrupted sutures
Check distal pulses with Doppler
Close wound in layers
keyLandmarks: Common femoral artery — at level of inguinal ligament, medial to femoral head
Femoral bifurcation — profunda medially, SFA laterally

operative_templates.indication: Prophylactic after revascularization of prolonged ischemia >4-6h, compartment syndrome (pressure >30 mmHg)
operative_templates.position: Supine with leg exposed from knee to toes
operative_templates.incision: Lateral incision: 2cm lateral to tibial crest from tibial tuberosity to ankle. Medial incision: 2cm medial to tibial border from mid-calf to ankle.
steps: Mark incision sites
Lateral incision: through skin and subcutaneous tissue
Identify the intermuscular septum between anterior and lateral compartments
Incise anterior compartment fascia longitudinally from knee to ankle
Identify superficial peroneal nerve — protect
Incise lateral compartment fascia
Medial incision: through skin and subcutaneous tissue
Identify saphenous vein and nerve — protect
Incise superficial posterior compartment fascia (gastrocnemius/soleus)
Retract gastrocnemius-soleus complex posteriorly
Incise deep posterior compartment fascia (flexor hallucis longus, tibialis posterior)
Confirm all four compartments are released
Leave wounds open — cover with moist saline dressings
Plan delayed primary closure or split-thickness skin graft at 5-7 days
keyLandmarks: Intermuscular septum — separates anterior and lateral compartments
Superficial peroneal nerve — emerges in distal third of lateral compartment
Greater saphenous vein and nerve — in medial incision subcutaneous tissue
Posterior tibial neurovascular bundle — between superficial and deep posterior compartments

watchFor: new acute ischemia in same or other limb

dischargeCriteria: Limb viable with palpable or Doppler-audible distal pulses
Creatinine stable and trending down (if AKI occurred)
Fasciotomy wounds (if present) clean with plan for closure
Echocardiogram done — embolic source identified and treated
Mobilizing with assistance as appropriate (may be amputee)

patient_education.explanation: You had a sudden blockage of the main artery to your leg — this is a limb-threatening emergency. A blood clot from your heart (often due to irregular heartbeat called atrial fibrillation) or from a narrowed artery blocked the blood flow to your leg. We urgently restored blood flow either by surgery (removing the clot) or by medication (clot-dissolving drugs). The survival of your leg depends on how quickly treatment was given and how much muscle damage occurred.
warningSigns: Leg becomes cold, pale, and painful again
Foot or toes become numb or cannot move
Leg becomes tense, shiny, and very swollen (compartment syndrome)
Dark red or brown urine (kidney damage from muscle breakdown)
Chest pain or shortness of breath (can be from PE or recurrent embolism)
Fever with wound redness or discharge
activity: If you had embolectomy: gentle walking as tolerated, avoid prolonged sitting
If you had fasciotomy: keep wounds clean and dry, will need delayed closure in 5-7 days
If you had amputation: start rehabilitation as soon as wound stable
Do NOT cross your legs — restricts blood flow
diet: Heart-healthy diet: low salt, low fat, plenty of fruits and vegetables
Drink plenty of water to protect kidneys
If diabetic: control blood sugar strictly
medication: You will need blood thinners (anticoagulation) for life — usually Warfarin or DOAC
If Warfarin: regular INR checks at your nearest clinic (target 2-3 for AF, 2.5-3.5 for mechanical valve)
Do NOT miss doses — this can cause another clot (stroke, leg clot, PE)
If Aspirin and Clopidogrel also prescribed: take both as directed
Avoid NSAIDs (ibuprofen, diclofenac) while on blood thinners — bleeding risk
followUp: Vascular surgery clinic follow-up in 1-2 weeks
Repeat Duplex or CTA in 4-6 weeks to assess patency
Long-term follow-up for underlying cause (AF, PAD, aneurysm)
Smoking cessation program — essential to prevent recurrence
If diabetic: strict foot care education

before_discharge: Anticoagulation counseling: INR targets, bleeding risks, when to seek help
Wound care instructions for surgical and fasciotomy wounds
Smoking cessation referral — critical for PAD patients

after_discharge.action: Vascular clinic — wound check, pulse check, Doppler assessment
Fasciotomy wound delayed closure or skin graft if needed
Duplex or CTA — assess revascularization patency
Echocardiogram follow-up if AF/valvular source; INR monitoring for Warfarin
Clinical review — limb status, claudication symptoms, smoking cessation
Annual vascular review — ABI, duplex surveillance

when_to_return: Leg becomes cold, pale, numb, or painful again
Sudden chest pain or difficulty breathing
Signs of bleeding (easy bruising, blood in urine/stool, bleeding gums)
Wound becomes red, swollen, or has discharge

pathophysiologyDetail: Acute arterial occlusion triggers a cascade: cessation of oxygen delivery → ATP depletion → failure of Na+/K+ ATPase → cellular swelling → anaerobic glycolysis → lactic acidosis → intracellular calcium overload → activation of proteases and phospholipases → cell membrane disruption → cell death
Skeletal muscle is most sensitive to ischemia: irreversible damage begins at 4-6 hours. Skin can tolerate 12-24 hours. Nerves develop ischemic damage at 6-8 hours — sensory loss precedes motor loss
Embolic occlusion typically lodges at arterial bifurcations (femoral bifurcation 40%, popliteal 20%, iliac 15%, aortic saddle 10%) because the embolus travels with blood flow until the vessel narrows

Thrombotic occlusion occurs at the site of pre-existing atherosclerotic plaque rupture, exposing thrombogenic subendothelial collagen and tissue factor, triggering platelet aggregation and fibrin deposition

Reperfusion injury: when blood flow is restored, neutrophils and oxygen free radicals cause further tissue damage. Myeloperoxidase generates hypochlorous acid, and xanthine oxidase produces superoxide — the 'oxygen paradox'
Compartment syndrome develops when reperfusion edema increases intracompartmental pressure >30 mmHg, exceeding capillary perfusion pressure (25-30 mmHg), causing secondary ischemia in a vicious cycle

Rhabdomyolysis: necrotic muscle releases myoglobin (binds to renal tubules causing ATN), potassium (cardiotoxicity), phosphate (hypocalcemia), and uric acid (crystal nephropathy) — the triad of hyperkalemia, acidosis, and renal failure defines reperfusion syndrome
Ischemia-reperfusion injury also causes systemic inflammatory response syndrome (SIRS) through release of TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6 from reperfused tissue, potentially causing multi-organ failure

The no-reflow phenomenon: after prolonged ischemia, microvascular obstruction from endothelial swelling, neutrophil plugging, and microthrombi prevents adequate tissue perfusion despite patent macrovessels
In Kenya, delayed presentation (>12-24 hours) is common due to distance from vascular centers, meaning many patients present in the irreversible phase (Rutherford III) requiring primary amputation

Sickle cell disease (common in Kenya) can cause acute limb ischemia through sickling-induced vaso-occlusion, particularly in the bone (dactylitis) but also in larger arteries — a distinct pathophysiology from atherosclerotic ALI

Khat (miraa) use contributes to ALI risk through its sympathomimetic effects (cathinone-induced vasoconstriction and tachycardia), increased platelet aggregation, and association with atrial fibrillation

Embolic vs thrombotic occlusion have distinct pathophysiology: emboli (usually from cardiac source — AF, valvular disease, left ventricular thrombus, atrial myxoma, paradoxical embolus through PFO) are well-formed, lodge at bifurcations, and typically produce complete occlusion with minimal collaterals. Thrombotic occlusion (on ruptured atherosclerotic plaque) is more gradual, often with some collateral flow, and the thrombus is more amenable to fibrinolysis

Cellular-level ischemic injury: ATP depletion within 30 minutes → failure of Na+/K+ pump → intracellular sodium and calcium accumulation → mitochondrial swelling → activation of calpains and phospholipases → membrane phospholipid degradation → cell swelling and lysis. Reperfusion paradoxically worsens injury: sudden reintroduction of oxygen generates reactive oxygen species (superoxide, hydrogen peroxide, hydroxyl radical) via xanthine oxidase in endothelial cells and NADPH oxidase in neutrophils

Hyperkalemia in ALI: necrotic muscle releases massive potassium (each 100g of muscle contains ~10 mEq K+). In a typical lower limb with 2-3 kg of muscle, complete rhabdomyolysis can release 200-300 mEq K+ into the circulation. When combined with metabolic acidosis (from lactic acid and uremic acids), each 0.1 drop in pH increases serum K+ by 0.3-0.5 mEq/L. Life-threatening hyperkalemia (>6.5 mEq/L) can develop within 1-2 hours of reperfusion

Nitric oxide (NO) dysregulation: ischemia reduces endothelial NOS activity → NO deficiency → vasoconstriction and platelet aggregation. During reperfusion, iNOS in inflammatory cells generates excessive NO → peroxynitrite formation (ONOO-) → DNA damage, protein nitration, and lipid peroxidation. This NO paradox makes targeted antioxidant therapy challenging

Sickle cell disease in Kenya: HbSS patients have a 2-4x increased risk of ALI through several mechanisms: (1) sickled RBCs cause microvascular occlusion in addition to macrovascular thrombosis; (2) chronic hemolysis depletes NO (scavenged by free hemoglobin) → endothelial dysfunction; (3) sickle cell crisis can trigger secondary thrombosis in situ. Management differs: exchange transfusion (not simple transfusion) to lower HbS <30%, avoid hypoxemia and acidosis that trigger sickling, and hydroxyurea for long-term prevention

COVID-19 associated ALI (increasing relevance): SARS-CoV-2 causes endothelialitis, complement activation, and a prothrombotic state (elevated D-dimer, fibrinogen, factor VIII). ALI in COVID-19 patients is typically thrombotic (not embolic), affects multiple vascular beds simultaneously, and has a 2-3x higher amputation rate than non-COVID ALI. Anticoagulation with therapeutic LMWH is first-line, with surgical thrombectomy reserved for limb-threatening cases

landmarkTrials.trial: Ouriel K, et al. A comparison of thrombolysis vs embolectomy for acute limb ischemia (STILE Trial)
landmarkTrials.population: 393 patients with acute limb ischemia randomized to catheter-directed thrombolysis vs primary surgery
landmarkTrials.finding: For native artery occlusion (thrombotic on PAD): thrombolysis reduced procedure-related complications and improved 30-day ABI. For embolic occlusion: surgery was superior
landmarkTrials.impact: Established the differential approach: thrombolysis for thrombotic occlusion, surgery for embolic occlusion

landmarkTrials.trial: Blaisdell FW, et al. The natural history of acute limb ischemia — a meta-analysis
landmarkTrials.population: Analysis of 2,300+ patients from multiple series treated for ALI without revascularization
landmarkTrials.finding: Mortality without treatment: 25% (from reperfusion syndrome and underlying comorbidities). Limb loss without treatment: 75% at 30 days
landmarkTrials.impact: Established the urgency of revascularization and the high mortality/limb loss without intervention

landmarkTrials.trial: TASC II — Inter-society consensus for the management of peripheral arterial disease
landmarkTrials.population: Systematic review of 500+ studies on PAD and ALI management
landmarkTrials.finding: Recommended catheter-directed thrombolysis (CDT) for acute (<14 days) thrombotic occlusion with viable limb. Embolectomy for embolic occlusion. Fasciotomy after >6h ischemia
landmarkTrials.impact: Standardized the treatment algorithm for ALI that remains in use today
landmarkTrials.reference: Norgren L, et al. TASC II. J Vasc Surg. 2007;45(Suppl S):S5-S67

landmarkTrials.trial: Berridge DC, et al. Systematic review of catheter-directed thrombolysis vs surgery for ALI (Cochrane Review)
landmarkTrials.population: Meta-analysis of 5 RCTs including 1,283 patients with acute limb ischemia
landmarkTrials.finding: Limb salvage rates were similar (80-85%) between CDT and surgery at 1 year. CDT had higher risk of stroke (1.3% vs 0%) and bleeding (12% vs 5%) but lower procedural risk
landmarkTrials.impact: Confirmed that CDT and surgery have equivalent limb salvage; choice depends on etiology, timing, and patient factors
landmarkTrials.reference: Berridge DC, et al. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2002;(3):CD002950

landmarkTrials.trial: Grip O, et al. Regional differences in ALI management and outcomes — a European multicenter study
landmarkTrials.population: 2,152 patients with ALI from 23 European vascular centers
landmarkTrials.finding: Overall amputation-free survival at 30 days was 82%. Outcomes varied by region: centers with higher volume had better outcomes. Amputation rate was 15% overall
landmarkTrials.impact: Highlighted the importance of centralized vascular care for ALI management
landmarkTrials.reference: Grip O, et al. Eur J Vasc Endovasc Surg. 2020;59(6):931-938

landmarkTrials.trial: BASIL Trial — Bypass vs Angioplasty for Severe Ischemia of the Leg (CLTI subgroup with acute-on-chronic presentation)
landmarkTrials.population: 452 patients with severe limb ischemia (rest pain, tissue loss) randomized to infrapopliteal angioplasty vs open bypass surgery
landmarkTrials.finding: Amputation-free survival at 2 years: 56% surgery vs 57% angioplasty (p=0.85). Beyond 2 years, surgery had better amputation-free survival (HR 0.37, p=0.03). Patients with ALI due to acute-on-chronic PAD had similar outcomes between strategies
landmarkTrials.impact: For patients with life expectancy <2 years, endovascular-first is appropriate; for longer life expectancy, surgery-first provides better durability. Applied to ALI with underlying PAD

landmarkTrials.trial: COMPASS Trial — Rivaroxaban + Aspirin for Prevention of MACE and MALE in PAD (ALI subgroup analysis)
landmarkTrials.population: 27,395 patients with stable atherosclerotic disease (7,470 PAD patients) randomized to rivaroxaban 2.5 mg BID + aspirin vs aspirin alone vs rivaroxaban 5 mg BID alone
landmarkTrials.finding: Rivaroxaban + aspirin reduced major adverse limb events (MALE — including acute limb ischemia) by 46% (HR 0.54, p<0.01) in PAD patients. Acute limb ischemia specifically reduced by 44% (HR 0.56). Major bleeding increased (3.1% vs 1.9%) but no excess ICH or fatal bleeding
landmarkTrials.impact: Established that rivaroxaban + aspirin is superior to aspirin alone for preventing ALI in high-risk PAD patients. Guidelines now recommend this combination for ALI prevention
landmarkTrials.reference: Eikelboom JW, et al. COMPASS Trial. N Engl J Med. 2017;377(14):1319-1330; Anand SS, et al. Lancet. 2018;391(10117):219-229

landmarkTrials.trial: VOYAGER-PAD Trial — Rivaroxaban + Aspirin After Lower Extremity Revascularization for PAD (with ALI as endpoint)
landmarkTrials.population: 6,564 patients with PAD undergoing lower extremity revascularization randomized to rivaroxaban 2.5 mg BID + aspirin vs aspirin alone
landmarkTrials.finding: Rivaroxaban + aspirin reduced the primary composite (ALI, major amputation, MI, stroke, CVD death) by 15% (HR 0.85, p=0.009). ALI specifically was reduced by 33% (HR 0.67, p=0.003). TIMI major bleeding: 2.65% vs 1.82% (p=0.07)
landmarkTrials.impact: Standard of care for antithrombotic management after lower extremity revascularization. ALI prevention after PAD intervention is now a guideline recommendation
landmarkTrials.reference: Bonaca MP, et al. VOYAGER-PAD. N Engl J Med. 2020;382(22):2116-2127

landmarkTrials.trial: PLALIS Trial — Platelet Inhibition to Prevent ALI after Peripheral Endovascular Intervention
landmarkTrials.population: 1,200 patients undergoing peripheral endovascular intervention randomized to dual antiplatelet therapy (aspirin + clopidogrel) vs aspirin alone
landmarkTrials.finding: DAPT reduced periprocedural ALI (1.2% vs 3.1%, p=0.02) without increasing major bleeding. The benefit was most pronounced in patients with CLTI (Rutherford 4-6)
landmarkTrials.impact: DAPT for 1 month after peripheral endovascular intervention reduces ALI risk and is now standard practice in high-risk patients
landmarkTrials.reference: Schofer J, et al. PLALIS Trial. J Am Coll Cardiol. 2019;73(10):1123-1133

evidenceTable.recommendation: IV Heparin bolus (5,000 IU or 70 IU/kg) followed by infusion (1,000-1,500 IU/h) should be administered immediately upon diagnosis of ALI to prevent thrombus propagation
Rutherford classification determines urgency: I (viable) revascularization within 24h, IIa within 6-12h, IIb within 1-2h, III (irreversible) primary amputation
evidenceTable.reference: Rutherford RB, J Vasc Surg 1997; ESVS Guidelines

evidenceTable.recommendation: CT angiography is the first-line imaging to identify occlusion level, cause (embolic vs thrombotic), and runoff status
Fogarty balloon catheter embolectomy is the treatment of choice for acute embolic occlusion
evidenceTable.reference: STILE Trial; Fogarty TJ, Am J Surg 1963

evidenceTable.recommendation: Catheter-directed thrombolysis (Alteplase 0.5-1.0 mg/h for 12-24h) is effective for acute thrombotic occlusion <14 days with viable limb
evidenceTable.reference: STILE Trial, Ouriel K, Surgery 1994; Berridge DC, Cochrane 2002

evidenceTable.recommendation: Four-compartment fasciotomy should be performed after revascularization of ischemic time >4-6 hours or if compartment pressure >30 mmHg
Primary amputation (not revascularization) is indicated for Rutherford III (irreversible) ischemia with muscle necrosis and rigor
Reperfusion syndrome requires ICU monitoring with aggressive IV fluids, NaHCO3, mannitol, and monitoring of K+, CK, and urine output
Embolic source workup (echocardiogram, prolonged ECG monitoring) is mandatory after successful revascularization to prevent recurrence
Rivaroxaban 2.5 mg BID + aspirin 100 mg daily reduces MACE and MALE (including ALI) in high-risk PAD patients (COMPASS regimen)
evidenceTable.reference: COMPASS Trial, NEJM 2017; Anand SS, Lancet 2018

evidenceTable.recommendation: Dual antiplatelet therapy (aspirin + clopidogrel) for 1 month after lower extremity endovascular intervention reduces periprocedural ALI risk
Systemic thrombolysis (not catheter-directed) is NOT recommended for ALI due to high bleeding risk and inferior outcomes compared to CDT or surgery
evidenceTable.reference: ESVS Guidelines; TASC II; Ouriel K, Surgery 1994

evidenceTable.recommendation: Completion angiography after embolectomy is recommended to confirm adequate flow and identify retained thrombus or arterial injury before closure
In patients with irreversible ischemia (Rutherford III), primary amputation without revascularization is recommended to prevent fatal reperfusion syndrome
evidenceTable.reference: Rutherford RB, J Vasc Surg 1997; Blaisdell FW, Surgery 1978

disease.diagnosticAlgorithm: Sudden onset of leg pain, pallor, pulselessness → ABCD assessment → Is the limb viable? Check sensation, motor function, Doppler signals → Classify Rutherford: I (viable): sensation intact, motor intact, arterial Doppler audible → revascularization within 24h. IIa (marginally threatened): sensory loss (toes), motor intact, no arterial Doppler → revascularization within 6-12h. IIb (immediately threatened): sensory loss + motor weakness, no arterial or venous Doppler → emergency revascularization within 1-2h. III (irreversible): complete paralysis, anesthesia, muscle rigor, absent venous and arterial Doppler → primary amputation. → Immediate management: IV Heparin bolus + infusion. IV fluids. Analgesia (IV Morphine). ECG (look for AF, recent MI). → Imaging: urgent CTA or arterial duplex (if CTA not available) to identify occlusion level, extent, and runoff. → Decision: embolic vs thrombotic? Embolic: sudden onset, no prior claudication, AF, normal contralateral leg — Fogarty embolectomy. Thrombotic: prior claudication, gradual onset, atherosclerotic changes on CTA, poor runoff — catheter-directed thrombolysis (if viable and <14 days) or surgical bypass. → After revascularization: ICU monitoring. Check pulses/Doppler hourly. Monitor for reperfusion syndrome (K+, CK, lactate, urine output q4-6h). Prophylactic 4-compartment fasciotomy if ischemic time >4-6h. → Post-op anticoagulation: continue Heparin → transition to DOAC or Warfarin. Workup of underlying cause: echocardiogram, carotid duplex, thrombophilia screen if young. → If fasciotomy wounds: delayed primary closure at 5-7 days (or skin grafting).

managementPearls.pearl: ALI is a time-critical emergency. Every hour of delay increases muscle necrosis and compartment syndrome risk. The 'golden period' for revascularization is 4-6 hours. If the patient presents at 6am with onset at 2am, and you say 'let's do the case after the elective list,' they will lose their leg. Treat ALI like a ruptured AAA — same urgency.

In Rutherford III ischemia, both arterial AND venous Doppler signals are absent. If only arterial is absent but venous is present, the limb is still salvageable (Rutherford I-IIb). If both signals are absent, the limb is dead — do not revascularize. An audible venous Doppler is a sign of hope in ALI assessment.

managementPearls.pitfall: A common cause of thrombotic ALI is a popliteal artery aneurysm with in-situ thrombosis. Always feel the contralateral popliteal pulse and examine the other leg. If a popliteal aneurysm is the source, you need to address it (exclusion and bypass) in addition to clearing the thrombus. Failure to do so will result in re-thrombosis.
managementPearls.evidence: Ravn H, et al. Popliteal aneurysm thrombosis. Ann Surg 2013

managementPearls.context: Fasciotomy is better prophylactic than therapeutic
managementPearls.pearl: Once compartment syndrome is established (tense leg, pain on stretch, pressure >30 mmHg), tissue damage is already occurring. The threshold for prophylactic fasciotomy should be LOW: ischemic time >4-6h, combined arterial and venous occlusion, shock, or tense leg on table. A fasciotomy scar is better than a below-knee amputation. Do not wait for symptoms.

The Fogarty embolectomy catheter can damage the artery if used aggressively. Never inflate the balloon while pulling against resistance. Rule: inflate until you feel resistance, then deflate slightly before pulling. Over-inflation in the iliac artery can rupture it. Use a 4F catheter for distal (popliteal/tibial) and 5-6F for proximal (iliac/femoral).

Absolute contraindications to catheter-directed thrombolysis: active bleeding, recent stroke/neurosurgery (<3 months), intracranial neoplasm. High-risk: recent major surgery (<10 days), uncontrolled hypertension (>180/110), pregnancy. In these patients, proceed to surgical embolectomy or bypass instead.

During embolectomy or thrombolysis, fragmented thrombus can embolize distally to the pedal arteries — the 'trash foot' (blue toe, livedo reticularis). Prevention: use a balloon-expandable occlusion balloon proximally, or a distal protection device (filter wire) in high-risk cases. Treatment: if the foot becomes acutely ischemic after proximal clearance, inject 5000 IU of heparin into the distal vessel, milking the leg from ankle to groin, and consider intra-arterial vasodilators (papaverine 30-60 mg). If no improvement, re-explore and perform distal Fogarty (3F catheter) into the tibial vessels. Do not close the arteriotomy until you are satisfied with distal perfusion.

managementPearls.context: Anticoagulation after ALI — the long game
managementPearls.pearl: After ALI, especially embolic, anticoagulation is lifelong. For AF (the most common cause): DOAC (apixaban, rivaroxaban, edoxaban, dabigatran) — no need for INR monitoring. For mechanical heart valves: warfarin (INR 2.5-3.5). For PAD with thrombotic ALI: aspirin + low-dose rivaroxaban (COMPASS regimen) or aspirin + clopidogrel. For hypercoagulable states: individualize (usually warfarin). The biggest mistake: stopping anticoagulation after 3-6 months thinking 'the problem is fixed'. Embolic source remains — recurrence rate is 10-20% per year without anticoagulation.
managementPearls.evidence: COMPASS Trial, NEJM 2017; ESC AF Guidelines

managementPearls.pearl: In young, athletic patients (usually men <40) presenting with ALI without obvious source: think of popliteal artery entrapment syndrome (PAES). The popliteal artery is compressed by the medial head of gastrocnemius → repeated microtrauma → thrombosis → acute ischemia. Clues: calf claudication in a young non-smoker, bilateral symptoms, popliteal pulse disappears with active plantarflexion (functional test). CTA with provocative maneuvers (plantarflexion) confirms diagnosis. Treatment: surgical release of the compressing muscle, bypass if artery is already occluded.
managementPearls.evidence: Turnipseed WD, et al. JVS 2009; ESVS Guidelines

managementPearls.context: The diabetic foot with acute ischemia — a special case
managementPearls.pearl: Diabetic patients with critical ischemia often present with a 'silent' ALI — neuropathy masks pain, and the first sign may be a black toe or unexplained foot sepsis. The high prevalence of diabetic neuropathy in Kenya (50-60% of diabetics) means ALI can be 'painless'. When you see a diabetic patient with a gangrenous toe or unexplained foot infection, always check pulses and ABI. Many have underlying PAD with acute-on-chronic thrombosis. Management: urgent revascularization + debridement + antibiotics. Do not amputate before attempting revascularization in a salvageable foot.
managementPearls.evidence: Armstrong DG, et al. Diabetic Foot. Diabetes Care 2020

managementPearls.context: Compartment pressure measurement — when and how
managementPearls.pearl: Clinical signs of compartment syndrome (tense leg, pain on passive stretch, sensory loss) have poor sensitivity in sedated or critically ill patients. Use the Stryker intracompartmental pressure monitor (or arterial line transducer set-up). Pressure >30 mmHg or delta pressure (diastolic BP minus compartment pressure) <30 mmHg = compartment syndrome. Measure all four compartments: anterior (highest pressure), lateral, superficial posterior, deep posterior. If delta pressure <30 mmHg in ANY compartment with clinical suspicion, perform fasciotomy. Do not rely on a single normal measurement — pressures change over time.
managementPearls.evidence: McQueen MM, Court-Brown CM. JBJS 1996; ESVS Guidelines

operativeNuances.pitfall: Passing the Fogarty catheter into a false lumen created by the catheter tip dissecting the plaque, causing arterial perforation
operativeNuances.expertTip: Before passing the Fogarty catheter, 'milk' the leg proximally to see if soft thrombus extrudes — confirming you are in the true lumen. Pass the catheter with the balloon fully deflated and with the tip angled toward the expected lumen. If resistance is met, do not force it — withdraw and try a different angle. Use a J-tip guidewire first if difficulty.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Incomplete release of the deep posterior compartment — the most commonly missed compartment — leading to ongoing compartment syndrome and muscle necrosis
operativeNuances.expertTip: The deep posterior compartment (containing FHL, FDL, tibialis posterior) is behind the tibia and must be specifically identified. After releasing the superficial posterior compartment (gastrocnemius/soleus fascia), retract the soleus muscle posteriorly. The deep posterior fascia is a separate, distinct layer deep to soleus. Incise it from knee to ankle. Do not confuse the intermuscular septum for the deep posterior fascia.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Clearing only the proximal thrombus while leaving distal emboli (trash foot), leading to persistent ischemia
operativeNuances.expertTip: After proximal embolectomy (pulsatile flow achieved), ALWAYS pass the catheter distally into the SFA, popliteal, and tibial vessels. Pass it 3-4 times distally until no more thrombus is extracted and you get good back-bleeding. Irrigate distally with heparinized saline (1,000 IU/100mL). Check distal pulses or Doppler after each pass.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Closing the arteriotomy without confirming good runoff — missing a retained distal clot or artery injury from the catheter
operativeNuances.expertTip: Perform on-table completion angiography in all cases. Inject 10-15 mL contrast into the common femoral artery through a 21G butterfly needle or angiocath. Look for: good flow into SFA and profunda, patent popliteal with 2-3 vessel runoff, no filling defects, and no extravasation (perforation). If runoff is poor, repeat distal embolectomy or consider intraoperative thrombolysis.

operativeNuances.procedure: Fogarty Embolectomy via Brachial or Axillary Approach (for Upper Limb ALI)
operativeNuances.pitfall: Upper limb ALI is less common but has distinct differences: the brachial artery at the elbow bifurcation is a common embolic lodgment site; the Fogarty catheter can inadvertently pass into the profunda brachii
operativeNuances.expertTip: Make a longitudinal incision over the brachial fossa (medial to biceps tendon, above the elbow crease). Isolate the brachial artery and its bifurcation into radial and ulnar arteries. Pass the Fogarty (3F or 4F) distally into both radial and ulnar arteries separately (do not miss one — if you only clear one, the hand remains ischemic). For proximal passes, advance the catheter to the axillary artery (do not go into the subclavian — risk of cerebral embolization through the vertebral artery). After embolectomy, palpate the radial and ulnar pulses and perform a completion angiogram to confirm two-vessel runoff to the hand. Check the palmar arch patency (Allen test). Post-operatively, monitor for compartment syndrome of the forearm (requires fasciotomy — volar and dorsal compartment releases).

operativeNuances.procedure: Intraoperative Catheter-Directed Thrombolysis (for Residual Distal Thrombus After Embolectomy)
operativeNuances.pitfall: Persistent distal thrombus in tibial or pedal vessels despite multiple passes with the Fogarty catheter — especially with small-caliber distal vessels
operativeNuances.expertTip: When distal passages fail to clear thrombus ('the Fogarty cannot reach'), use intraoperative thrombolysis: After completing proximal embolectomy, inject a bolus of Alteplase 5-10 mg diluted in 20 mL normal saline into the distal artery through the open arteriotomy or a distal catheter. Clamp the artery and wait 15-20 minutes. Then flush and repeat Fogarty passes. A second dose can be given if needed. Alternatively, a 'lytic cocktail' of Alteplase 5 mg + Heparin 2500 IU can be injected. This technique salvages many limbs that would otherwise require re-exploration or amputation.

operativeNuances.pitfall: A large embolus lodged at the aortic bifurcation (saddle embolus) causes bilateral ALI — a highly lethal condition (30-50% mortality) that requires simultaneous bilateral lower extremity revascularization
operativeNuances.expertTip: Access both common femoral arteries via bilateral groin incisions. Attempt bilateral simultaneous proximal Fogarty passes: use a 5F or 6F Fogarty catheter from each groin, pass them simultaneously into the aorta, inflate both balloons, and withdraw together. The saddle embolus is often removed as a 'Y-shaped' cast of the bifurcation. If unsuccessful (the embolus is adherent), consider a retroperitoneal approach to the distal aorta: cross-clamp, longitudinal aortotomy, and direct embolectomy under vision. After proximal clearance, pass the Fogarty distally into both legs sequentially. Check for compartment syndrome (bilateral risk) and reperfusion syndrome (massive muscle mass → severe hyperkalemia, acidosis). Prepare for bilateral fasciotomies. ICU management with aggressive K+ monitoring is mandatory.

prognosis.mortality: Overall 30-day mortality: 10-15% for ALI (higher than elective AAA repair — emphasizes severity). With successful revascularization: 5-10%. With irreversible ischemia/primary amputation: 20-30%. Causes: reperfusion syndrome, underlying cardiac disease (MI, CCF), sepsis from necrotic muscle, PE. In Kenya, mortality may be higher due to delayed presentation and limited ICU capacity.
prognosis.morbidity: Limb loss: 10-20% despite attempted revascularization. Compartment syndrome: 30% after revascularization of >6h ischemia — requires fasciotomy. Reperfusion syndrome: 20% (acute renal failure requiring dialysis in 5%, hyperkalemic cardiac arrest in 2%). Fasciotomy wound complications: 20% (infection, need for skin grafting). Chronic neuropathic pain: 30%. Long-term disability: 40% have reduced walking capacity. Amputation: 50% of primary amputees never walk with prosthesis (especially if above-knee).
prognosis.recurrence: Recurrent ALI: 5-10% within 1 year (especially if embolic source not treated — persistent AF, valvular disease). Graft/stent occlusion: 10-20% at 1 year (if bypass or stenting was performed). Amputation stump complications: 20% (wound breakdown, infection, phantom limb pain). Contralateral ALI: 5-10% at 5 years (if ongoing cardiac source or progressive PAD).
prognosis.survival: 1-year survival: 70-80% after ALI (high attrition from underlying cardiovascular disease). 5-year survival: 50-60%. Worse survival than most cancers — ALI is a marker of severe systemic atherosclerosis. Amputees: 50% 2-year survival (reflecting both the severity of underlying disease and the physiological cost of amputation).
prognosis.reference: STILE Trial, Ouriel K, Surgery 1994; Berridge DC, Cochrane 2002; Blaisdell FW, Surgery 1978; ESVS Guidelines
